ranking.design_name: StateTrace (Procedural Code-Execution / Output-Prediction over a Bounded Deterministic DSL)
ranking.rationale: STRONGEST overall and the safest moat. (1) DISCRIMINATION CONFIDENCE: highest of the four. The anchor (CRUXEval-O) is VERIFIED unsaturated at the frontier — GPT-4 CoT 81.9%, GPT-4.1 ~77% (crux-eval.github.io; arXiv 2401.03065; academic re-runs 2025), well below ceiling, and small/mid coders spread cleanly (Codestral-22B ~50%, Qwen2.5-Coder-32B ~61-62%). Execution-step/state-mutation DEPTH is the brief's #2 durable lever and CRUXEval/InfiniteBench Code.Run prove it bites. (2) GOLD CORRECTNESS IS BULLETPROOF: gold = stock CPython output. Unlike ConstraintForge/ChromaLadder, there is NO hand-written verifier that can be subtly wrong and NO solver to integrate — the oracle is the reference interpreter itself, the exact CRUXEval recipe (MIT, VERIFIED facebookresearch/cruxeval). This collapses the dominant risk that sinks the other designs. (3) SCORING is trivial and surface-insensitive (ast.literal_eval + ==). (4) CONSTRAINT-2 SAFE by the brief's own sanction: only the harness runs ITS OWN generated program at generation time; the model emits a literal string that is parsed, never executed. (5) ORTHOGONALITY is genuine — LiveCodeBench's own data shows code-execution reorders models differently from code-gen/math. RESIDUAL RISK is real but the MOST measurable of the four: the anti-shortcut filter must guarantee path-dependence (else a closed-form collapse saturates the top, the exact v0 failure mode). This is testable pre-launch via the two adversarial baselines ('predict last literal', 'sum all int literals' near chance) + the CoT-vs-no-CoT gap (CRUXEval's +30pt trace finding) + IRT pruning. Build effort 2-4 wks is justified because the oracle is free. Minor FLAG: the design cites DyCodeEval for '5 published AST transforms' — DyCodeEval is actually an LLM multi-agent rewriter; the gold-preserving AST-transform idea is from the VarNorm/ConstUnfold line ('Is Your Benchmark Still Useful?'). Doesn't affect the design (transforms are reimplementable and sound).

STRONGEST EVIDENCE BASE and the highest-credibility/lowest-effort of the four — the right CO-pilot with StateTrace. (1) DISCRIMINATION CONFIDENCE is the best-evidenced: VERIFIED from the IFBench paper itself (arXiv 2507.02833, NeurIPS 2025) that leading models 'such as Qwen3-32B or Claude 4 Sonnet score below 50%' on OOD-stacked constraints despite 80%+ on IFEval — a real ~20-30pp frontier gap on EXACTLY this skill. Multiplicative ALL-PASS decay (p^N) is a sound, legible discrimination engine, and per-constraint pass-rate gives a graded floor so 1-3B locals are not pinned at 0. (2) LOWEST EFFORT/RISK to launch (1.5-2.5 wks): verifier patterns are portable from EleutherAI lm-eval IFEval (Apache-2.0) and the loose-cleaning algorithm is published. (3) LICENSES VERIFIED CLEAN: IFEval Apache-2.0, IFBench code Apache-2.0 + data ODC-BY-1.0 — and the design ships only its own generator, so even those are belt-and-suspenders. (4) ORTHOGONAL to StateTrace (constraint-tracking-while-generating vs interpreter-simulation are disjoint skills) and to the adopted axes. (5) Accuracy-% headline is leaderboard-LEGIBLE. TWO REAL CAVEATS that keep it #2 not #1: (a) CONTAMINATION/GOODHART — the SAME IFBench paper shows IF-RLVR training lifts models past 50% (Tülu-3-8B 28.9->45.9; Qwen2.5-7B->54.7), i.e. this skill is DIRECTLY trainable, so a lab that RL's on verifiable constraints could climb our ladder without general gain; the private+rotated OOD composite pool is the essential mitigation and must be genuinely large/fresh. (b) Same verifier-is-gold fragility as ChromaLadder (a buggy sentence-splitter = false gold), but it is more contained here (constraints are simple, IFEval verifiers are battle-tested) and the AutoIF-style unit-test+fuzz gate is well-trodden. FLAG: the assignment's 'top models <50% on IFBench' is corroborated by the PAPER but CONTRADICTED by the live Artificial Analysis leaderboard (82-83% top, under suspect names like 'Grok 4.3'); trust the paper's <50%, treat AA's high scores as post-RLVR/confabulated.

SOUND and the most durable difficulty lever in principle (NP-complete search, the brief's #1 lever), but THIRD because of (a) overlap risk with the suite's existing combinatorial-CSP axis and (b) a sharp, design-acknowledged shortcut hazard. (1) ANCHORS VERIFIED: ZebraLogic (N!)^M search space + Small..X-Large(>=1e10) categories + 'curse of complexity' collapse past ~1e9 (arXiv 2502.01100), NPHardEval P->NP-hard degradation (arXiv 2312.14890), AutoLogi open-ended 35-73% vs MCQ 21-37% spread + verifier F1 0.96 vs judge 0.76 (arXiv 2502.16906) — all confirm the recipe. Generator-first + solver-as-oracle + pin-a-clique for a literal-unique answer is correct, and verification asymmetry (check O(n+E), solve NP-complete) is airtight. Effort is LOW (4-6 days; prototype already brute-verifies count==1). (2) WHY ONLY SUPPLEMENTARY: the design ITSELF concedes the biggest risk — its uniqueness-forcing 'adjacent to k-1 colors' gadgets make many vertices single-step unit-propagation/DSATUR-solvable, so a POLY HEURISTIC can crack the nominal k^n space and silently saturate the top (the v0 failure mode). Mitigation (generate near the phase transition, DSATUR baseline gate, deepen forcing) is real but turns the easy 'low-effort' story into genuine tuning work, and the fallback (switch hard tier to chromatic-NUMBER optimization) complicates the scorer. (3) ORTHOGONALITY CAVEAT: if the suite ALSO adopts ZebraLogic as its combinatorial axis, this double-counts; the design's own decision rule (ship exactly one of ZebraLogic/ChromaLadder unless pilot item-level r<0.7) is the right gate. As a SECOND own-axis it is strong; as a launch headline it is riskier and less differentiated than StateTrace/ConstraintForge. Verdict: pilot it AFTER the top two, primarily to decide ChromaLadder-vs-adopt-ZebraLogic for the CSP slot.

MOST ORIGINAL and the only axis that measures 'knows-what-it-doesn't-know' — genuinely differentiated and judge-free — but the LEAST launch-ready, and the design AUTHOR HONESTLY says so ('most likely a strong V2 axis rather than a launch headline'). Agree. (1) UNPROVEN CORE PREMISE (the dominant risk, self-identified): that self-consistency confidence beats verbalized confidence at AURC AND tracks calibration BEYOND what raw accuracy/difficulty explain. If self-consistency is just a difficulty proxy, AbstainBench re-measures the obscurity ladder, not a distinct faculty. AURC + self-consistency are VERIFIED as established, legitimate selective-prediction tools (multiple 2024-2026 calibration papers), but I could NOT verify the specific load-bearing figure (arXiv 2509.01455, 'K=1 raises AURC 6-10%') — treat as plausible-but-unverified. (2) COST: k=6 inference/item (6x) for a metric (AURC) that is LESS legible to a leaderboard audience than an accuracy %. (3) DETECTOR FRAGILITY: FP/UA correctness rides on frozen pushback/abstention regex hitting >=0.9 precision/recall across verbose hedging — checkable on the 200-item fixture but a real failure surface. (4) STRENGTHS that keep it 'supplementary' not 'skip': license posture is the cleanest (Wikidata CC0 seed; correctly takes only the design idea from KG-FPQ/MIT and (QA)^2/Apache-2.0, and explicitly does NOT redistribute FalseQA which has no license — VERIFIED KG-FPQ repo exists and uses an LLM FPQ-Judge, so the judge-free reimplementation is the right call); the paired AC/FP/UA design structurally defeats blanket-abstain and blanket-answer; and hallucination-discipline is a marketable axis no accuracy-only competitor offers. VERDICT: build the construct-validity probe (verbalized-vs-self-consistency, E-AURC to factor out accuracy) as a cheap pre-pilot; promote to a shipped 'calibration' axis ONLY if self-consistency separates calibrated from confidently-wrong beyond accuracy. Not a launch differentiator.

recommended_pilot: Pilot StateTrace and ConstraintForge FIRST, in parallel. They are the two highest-confidence, most-orthogonal, leaderboard-legible axes, and together they cover two of the brief's most durable levers with the cleanest gold: StateTrace owns execution-step/state-mutation depth (gold = stock CPython, no fragile verifier, no solver — the lowest gold-correctness risk of all four, anchored to a VERIFIED-unsaturated CRUXEval-O at ~77-82% frontier), and ConstraintForge owns compositional constraint-stacking (the best-EVIDENCED discrimination of the four — the IFBench paper itself confirms Claude 4 Sonnet / Qwen3-32B <50% with a ~20-30pp gap vs IFEval, and it is the lowest-effort to stand up at ~1.5-2.5 weeks). They measure disjoint skills (a model can ace CSP/tracing yet drop 8 formatting rules, and vice-versa), so piloting both maximises information about whether an own-axis discriminates at all. Hold ChromaLadder as the very next pilot — but scope it as a decision between ChromaLadder and simply adopting ZebraLogic for the single combinatorial-CSP slot (run the cross-bench item-level correlation; ship one only if r<0.7). Defer AbstainBench to a cheap construct-validity probe, not a full build.
smallest_high_signal_pilot: A 2-axis, ~3-engineer-day discrimination smoke test BEFORE any launch-quality build — designed to kill or confirm fast, directly answering the v0 'non-discriminating' failure. SCOPE: (1) StateTrace: generate ~120 items across 3 rungs (S=3-6 floor / 12-25 mid / 40-120 frontier, 40 each) using a minimal restricted-CPython tracer (gold = subprocess return repr) + the two adversarial baselines ('predict-last-literal', 'sum-all-int-literals') wired from the start. (2) ConstraintForge: ~120 items across N=1 / N=3-4 / N=7-9 (with ~10 OOD composites at the hard rung), reusing lm-eval's IFEval loose-cleaning + ~15 in-distribution verifiers, each with 4 unit tests. MODEL PANEL (5, fixed seeds): one 1-1.5B (e.g. Qwen2.5-1.5B), one 7-8B (Qwen2.5-Coder-7B or Llama-3.1-8B), one 14B, plus TWO frontier anchors via API (one Claude-class, one GPT/Gemini-class) on the IDENTICAL items. SUCCESS = ALL of: (a) MONOTONE decline in accuracy/ALL-PASS as the knob (S / N) rises, for every model — proves the lever, not noise, drives difficulty; (b) a frontier-vs-1B SPREAD of >=30pp on the hard rung AND a non-zero, separated floor (1B clearly beats random; 7B clearly beats 1B) on the easy/mid rungs — proves it is neither saturated nor floored across 1-14B->frontier; (c) the best frontier anchor lands BELOW ceiling on the hard rung (target <=75% StateTrace, <=70% ConstraintForge ALL-PASS), matching CRUXEval-O/IFBench's verified-unsaturated profiles; (d) both adversarial baselines score near chance on StateTrace and a 'restate-the-prompt' degenerate fails ConstraintForge ALL-PASS — proves no trivial shortcut; (e) point-biserial item discrimination computable with a healthy spread (drop r<0.15 items). FAILURE/KILL: if a frontier anchor saturates a hard rung (StateTrace anti-shortcut filter leaked a closed form; or ConstraintForge feasibility too loose), OR a 1B already passes a hard rung, the lever is not biting — turn the knob (deepen aliasing/mutation density for StateTrace; raise N/OOD fraction for ConstraintForge) and re-probe before committing engineering weeks. This ~$ low-cost run (short prompts, single sample except none needed) yields go/no-go on the moat thesis itself, not just on one design."
overall_verdict: An own-benchmark SHOULD be a CORE moat — but a TWO-axis spine (StateTrace + ConstraintForge), not a single bet, and only AFTER the discrimination smoke test passes. REASONING: (1) The v0 suite failed precisely because ASSEMBLED benches were non-discriminating (9B ~= SOTA; Gemini > Opus), and the brief's verified anchors show that PROCEDURAL, generator-owns-the-gold designs with durable levers do NOT collapse at the frontier in 2026: CRUXEval-O is verified unsaturated (~77-82%), and IFBench's OWN PAPER puts Claude 4 Sonnet / Qwen3-32B <50% on stacked OOD constraints. Procedural regeneration is also the only contamination posture that survives a leaderboard's incentive to train-to-the-test — the strongest tier of the brief's defence ordering. So the own-benchmark directly fixes both failures (discrimination + contamination) that the adopted axes cannot, which is the definition of a moat. (2) BUT not every axis is moat-grade. The genuine moat is the pair that combines (i) durable lever, (ii) bulletproof gold, and (iii) leaderboard legibility: StateTrace (gold = CPython, zero verifier-correctness risk, depth lever) and ConstraintForge (best-evidenced frontier gap, lowest effort, accuracy-% legible). ChromaLadder is a strong THIRD/supplementary axis whose value is mostly as the ChromaLadder-vs-adopt-ZebraLogic decision for ONE combinatorial slot (ship one, not both, unless pilot r<0.7). AbstainBench is a differentiated-but-unproven V2 axis (6x cost, illegible AURC, unverified self-consistency-beats-verbalized premise) — supplementary at best until its construct-validity probe passes. (3) HONEST RISK on the core thesis: every own-axis lives or dies on a pre-launch quality gate (StateTrace's anti-shortcut filter; ConstraintForge's verifier-correctness + private OOD rotation against the IF-RLVR trainability shown in the IFBench paper). These are MEASURABLE pre-launch (adversarial baselines + IRT + frontier-anchor saturation check), which is exactly why the smallest-pilot smoke test is mandatory FIRST: it converts the moat from a hope into a tested claim. Net: build the own-benchmark as the core differentiator, lead with StateTrace + ConstraintForge, gate on the 3-day discrimination pilot, and keep adopted axes (SuperGPQA/IFBench/math/long-context/agentic) as the breadth around the proprietary spine.
